coredata.dc:description:
Photocatalysis is a green and efficient technology for degradation of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) in water, and development of novel photocatalyst with high activity is urgently needed. In this study, a class of composite photocatalysts (KT-xCQDs-yT), i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated K2Ti6O13 nanotube (KTNT), were prepared via a facile hydrothermal treatment combined with calcination. The optimized photocatalyst (KT-2.0–300) exhibited high photocatalytic activity for naproxen (NPX) degradation, achieving 100 % degradation efficiency within 20 min. In addition, the pseudo-first-order rate constant (k 1) for KT-2.0–300 was ∼52 times higher than that for neat KTNT without CQDs decoration. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further demonstrate that due to the introduction of CQDs, the electrons of Ti 3d are in a spin polarized state in KTNT, which greatly inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, CQDs can receive electrons from O 2p and transfer them to Ti 3d, which further improves the electron transfer efficiency and promotes reactive species generation. Photogenerated holes (h
+ ) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) were demonstrated the primary reactive species during NPX degradation. Then based on identification of degradation intermediates and DFT calculation on Fukui index of NPX, the speculative pathway of NPX degradation involved three primary routes: demethoxylation, decarboxylation and dehydroxylation. Deep degradation of NPX in the photocatalysis system also leads to efficient detoxification of NPX. This study presents a feasible method for synthesis of titanate-based photocatalysts with significant potential for the removal of PPCPs from wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Photocatalysis is a green and efficient technology for degradation of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) in water, and development of novel photocatalyst with high activity is urgently needed. In this study, a class of composite photocatalysts (KT-xCQDs-yT), i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated K2Ti6O13 nanotube (KTNT), were prepared via a facile hydrothermal treatment combined with calcination. The optimized photocatalyst (KT-2.0–300) exhibited high photocatalytic activity for naproxen (NPX) degradation, achieving 100 % degradation efficiency within 20 min. In addition, the pseudo-first-order rate constant (k 1) for KT-2.0–300 was ∼52 times higher than that for neat KTNT without CQDs decoration. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further demonstrate that due to the introduction of CQDs, the electrons of Ti 3d are in a spin polarized state in KTNT, which greatly inhibits the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. In addition, CQDs can receive electrons from O 2p and transfer them to Ti 3d, which further improves the electron transfer efficiency and promotes reactive species generation. Photogenerated holes (h
+ ) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) were demonstrated the primary reactive species during NPX degradation. Then based on identification of degradation intermediates and DFT calculation on Fukui index of NPX, the speculative pathway of NPX degradation involved three primary routes: demethoxylation, decarboxylation and dehydroxylation. Deep degradation of NPX in the photocatalysis system also leads to efficient detoxification of NPX. This study presents a feasible method for synthesis of titanate-based photocatalysts with significant potential for the removal of PPCPs from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) have emerged as significant environmental contaminants due to their adverse health effects to eco-system and human beings [1]. Naproxen (NPX) is a widely used pharmaceutical with potent anti-inflammatory, antipyretic and analgesic properties [2]. NPX will release to aquatic environments primarily through wastewater discharge and improper disposal by manufacturers [3]. Despite typically being detected at low concentrations (∼25 ng/L – 35 μg/L) in the effluents of wastewater treatment plants, NPX poses potential risks to environmental sustainability and human health due to its poorly understood long-term effects [4]. However, the low biodegradability of NPX renders conventional wastewater treatment methods ineffective for its removal [5]. Consequently, there is a growing focus on developing advanced and effective techniques for NPX removal from wastewaters.

Heterogeneous catalysis using functional materials has demonstrated to show high efficiency in the removal of PPCPs [6]. Among all the catalytic methods, photocatalysis stands out as an environmental-friendly and energy-efficient approach due to its potential for harnessing solar energy [7]. Nanostructured titanium-based materials are well-recognized photocatalysts, considering their capability to degrade a wide range of refractory organic compounds including NPX [8]. Specifically, potassium titanate nanotube (KTNT), with a chemical structure of K2Ti6O13, is reported as a prominent titanium-based photocatalyst, which has garnered increasing attention owing to the unique tunnel crystal structure, chemical stability, intrinsic optical properties, and electronic characteristics [9]. KTNT is synthesized through an alkali-thermal process, during which TiO2 microspheres undergo a curled transformation into well-defined tubular architectures [10]. This one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure significantly enhances photocatalytic efficiency by providing an enlarged specific surface area then TiO2 that facilitates pollutant adsorption and exposes abundant active sites [11]. Compared to other titanate nanotubes (e.g., sodium titanate and hydrogen titanate), the larger ionic radius of K⁺ (relative to H⁺ and Na⁺) enhances its efficacy as an electron donor, thereby optimizing charge separation during photocatalysis [10]. Concurrently, the interlayer spacing of titanate nanotubes is expanded through K⁺ incorporation, which facilitates electron transport kinetics and suppresses photogenerated electron-hole recombination [10]. However, the application of KTNT in photocatalysis is constrained by its activation requirement of ultraviolet (UV) light, which limits the full utilization of solar energy [12]. Thus, the photocatalytic efficiency of neat KTNT is hindered by the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [13], and modification of KTNT to improve its photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation is urgently needed.

As a novel addition to the carbon nanomaterials family, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have rapidly gained attention due to their versatile properties. CQDs exhibit excellent aqueous solubility, chemical inertness, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable photoluminescence, leading to their widespread application in bioimaging, biosensing, drug delivery, and electrocatalysis [14]. In recent years, CQDs have also been integrated into photocatalytic technologies [15]. These quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles, typically smaller than 10 nm in diameter, are composed of amorphous or nanocrystalline sp2-hybridized carbons, diamond-like sp3-hybridized carbons, and various surface functional groups (such as C–O, CO and C–OH) [16]. The π–π* transition of the CC bond and the n–π* transition in the CO bond endow CQDs with strong optical absorption in the UV light region, extending into the visible spectrum [17]. Notably, CQDs exhibit remarkable up-converted photoluminescence, enabling them to enhance the solar energy utilization range of wide band-gap nanophotocatalysts, shifting from UV to the visible region [16]. Additionally, the conjugated carbon structure of CQDs facilitates their role as efficient electron acceptors, reservoirs, and transfer agents, effectively reducing the recombination probability of photogenerated charges within nanophotocatalysts [18]. Recent studies have reported enhanced photocatalytic activity in various CQDs-modified composite photocatalysts, including CQDs/TiO2 [19], CQDs/g-C3N4 [20], CQDs/BiOBr [21], CQDs/Cu2O [22], CQDs/Bi2WO6 [18], CQDs/KNbO3 [23]. Building on this foundation, the development of CQDs-decorated K2Ti6O13 nanotubes (CQDs/KTNT) offers a promising approach to achieve enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance for NPX.

In this work, novel photocatalysts of CQDs-decorated KTNT (labeled as KT-xCQDs-yT) were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment combined with subsequent calcination, which were applied for photocatalytic degradation of NPX. The specific objectives of this study were: (1) to fabricate and characterize the newly developed KT-xCQDs-yT composite photocatalysts; (2) to investigate the photocatalytic removal efficiency of NPX from water, and propose the degradation pathway based on transformation products (TPs) identification results and theoretical calculations; (3) to reveal the underlying mechanism on the enhanced photocatalytic activity of KT-xCQDs-yT composite compared with the original KTNT; (4) to elucidate the decoration of CQDs induced electron spin polarization in Ti 3d orbitals in KTNT for enhanced photocatalytic activity. Overall, this study presents a feasible method for synthesis of KT-xCQDs-yT photocatalyst with significant potential for the effective removal of PPCPs from contaminated water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: All chemicals used in the experiments were of analytical grade or higher, and deionized (DI) water was used as solvent to prepare solution. Details on chemicals are presented in Text S1 of Supplementary data (SD).

The CQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal treatment process, as described in previous research [14]. Specifically, 6.3042 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved in 60 mL of DI water, followed by the addition of 2.01 mL of ethylenediamine. This solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined reactor and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. After allowing the reactor to cool naturally to room temperature, the resulting dark brown CQDs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C.

Neat KTNT was synthesized through a facile alkali-hydrothermal treatment process [24]. Specifically, 1.2 g of TiO2 was dispersed in 66.7 mL of a 10 mol/L KOH solution. Afterwards, the mixture was subjected to magnetic stirring for 12 h and then transferred to a Teflon-lined reactor, where it was heated to 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting white product was subsequently washed with a 0.01 mol/L hydrochloric acid solution until the pH reached approximately 7.0, followed by oven-drying at 105 °C for 12 h.

The CQDs-decorated KTNT (KT-xCQDs-yT) were synthesized through a combined hydrothermal treatment and subsequent calcination processes. Firstly, 0.2 g of KTNT was dispersed in 50 mL of DI water under magnetic stirring. A specified volume of CQDs solution was then added to the dispersion. After that, the mixture was stirred continuously for 12 h at room temperature, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The resulting precipitate was washed three times with DI water and then dried at 105 °C for 12 h. The dried powder was subsequently subjected to heating at specific temperatures for 2 h. The CQDs content in KT-xCQDs-yT was varied, with added volume of 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, and 2.5 mL. The calcination temperature was set at 200, 300, and 400 °C, respectively. In this study, the prepared photocatalysts are denoted as KT-x-y, where “x” represents the added volume of CQDs and “y” indicates the calcination temperature. The notation KT-0–0 refers to the neat KTNT without CQDs addition and calcination treatment.

Details of the material characterization techniques are described in Text S2 of SD, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area analysis, diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) and zeta potential analysis. The photoelectrical properties of materials were measured on electrochemical workstation (CHI660, China) equipped with PEC2000 light (Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd. China), and the detailed information is described in Text S3 of SD.

Batch photocatalytic experiments were conducted in a 250 mL double-layer quartz reactor maintained at 25 °C using a circulating cooling water system. A solar simulator (300 W Xe arc lamp, 80 mW/cm2, Microsolar300, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., Ltd. China) served as the light source, which was operated at AM1.5 mode. In each experiment, 100 mL of a 5 μmol/L NPX (physiochemical properties shown in Table S1 of SD) solution was introduced into the quartz reactor. Subsequently, 0.02 g of the prepared photocatalyst was dispersed into the NPX solution. The pH of the reaction mixture was then adjusted to 5.0 using 0.01 mol/L HClO4 or KOH solution. Before initiating light irradiation, the mixture was stirred for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and NPX. During the photocatalytic degradation of NPX, 1 mL of the mixture was sampled every 5 min and immediately filtered through 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane filter for further concentration analysis.

The concentration of NPX was monitored using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, Agilent 1260, USA). Detailed information on the NPX quantification method using HPLC is provided in Text S4 of SD. The photocatalytic degradation process of NPX was described by the pseudo-first-order kinetic model [25]:

where C 0 and C
t (mg/L) are the NPX concentrations at initial time and real-time t (min), respectively; k 1 (min−1) is the pseudo-first-order rate constant.

Scavenger quenching experiments were performed to identify the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during the photocatalytic reaction. Specifically, 50 mmol/L of ammonium oxalate (AO), isopropanol (IPA), and tiron (TI) were individually added to the reaction system to quench photo-generated holes (h⁺), hydroxyl radicals (•OH), and superoxide radicals (•O2 -), respectively [20,26,27]. The detection of ROS in water and spin polarization of solid materials during the reaction were conducted on an electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectrometer (EMXplus-6/1, Bruker, Germany), and detailed parameter settings are shown in Text S5 of SD. In addition, magnets were used to investigate the effect of applied magnetic field (12 mT) for NPX removal in the photocatalysis system, which were placed close to the reactor (1 cm away). Meanwhile, the intensity of magnetic field was measured by a digital tesla meter (VC862A, Shenzhen Yisheng Victor Tech Co., Ltd, China).

To evaluate the effect of pH, pH of solution was adjusted from 3.0 to 11.0 by diluted HClO4 (0.1 M) and KOH (0.1 M). Effects of other water chemistry factors including coexisting ions (Ca2+, SO4 2-, HCO3 - and PO4 3-, sodium forms), natural organic matter (humic acid, HA) and initial NPX concentrations on NPX degradation in this photocatalysis system using KT-2.0–300 were evaluated. To assess the reusability of developed photocatalyst, five consecutive recycling tests were conducted for NPX degradation. After each cycle, the photocatalyst was separated by filtering through a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane, followed by washing with DI water and ethanol for three times. The recovered sample was then dried at 105 °C for 3 h before being reused in the subsequent NPX degradation cycle. To assess the contribution of visible light to the photocatalytic degradation, a UV cut-off filter (λ < 420 nm) was positioned between the light source and the reactor. The intermediates formed during NPX photodegradation were analyzed on an ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC, Dionex UltiMate 3000 Series) coupled with an Orbitrap mass spectrometer (MS, Thermo Scientific, USA). Detailed procedures for intermediate detection using LC-MS are provided in Text S6 of SD.

To elucidate the photocatalytic degradation pathway of NPX, computational calculations were performed based on density functional theory (DFT) by the B3LYP/6–31 G* computational methods on Gaussian 16 C.01 software [28]. The active sites of NPX molecule during the reaction were identified by evaluating the condensed Fukui index [29]. Additional details on the DFT calculations are provided in Text S7 of SD.

To elucidate the synergistic photocatalytic effect of CQDs and KTNT, DFT calculations were using the projector-augmented-wave (PAW) potentials with Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) of exchange-correlation interactions, which were implemented in the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP, version 6.5.0) code [30–32]. The PAW potentials were used to describe the ionic cores and valence electrons were taken into account using a plane wave basis set with a kinetic energy cutoff of 450 eV [33,34]. Partial occupancies of the Kohn-Sham orbitals were allowed using the Gaussian smearing method with a width of 0.05 eV. The electronic energy was considered self-consistent when the energy change was smaller than 10−5 eV, and a geometry optimization was considered convergent when the force change was smaller than −0.02 eV/Å. Grimme’s DFT-D3 methodology was used to describe the dispersion interactions [35]. The Brillourin zone was sampled with a gamma-centered grid of 14 × 14 × 6 (K2Ti6O13) and 3 × 3 × 2 (CQDs-decorated K2Ti6O13) through all the computational processes [36]. In addition, the Materials Studio 2023 is used to aid the model construction. The VASPKIT 1.5.1 package was employed to aid in the analysis of electronic structures [37]. The charge density difference (CDD) is calculated as follows:
(2) △ρ=ρtot(r)−ρ(K2Ti6O13)−ρ(CQDs)
where ρtot(r), ρ(K2Ti6O13) and ρ(CQDs) are the charge distribution of total system, K2Ti6O13 and CQDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, composition and crystal structure of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1a shows the synthesis process of CQDs-decorated KTNT photocatalysts. The neat KTNT (i.e., KT-0–0, Fig. 1b) exhibits a multiwalled tubular structure, with lengths of several hundred nanometers and diameters around 10 nm. Fig. 1c shows that KT-2.0–300, the optimized photocatalyst confirmed in the next section, retains the tubular structure, indicating that the decoration of CQDs onto nanotubes did not alter the primary morphology of the KTNT. Compared with neat KTNT, several nanodots with a diameter of ∼10 nm in were observed on the surface of the KTNT in the KT-2.0–300 composite, suggesting successful deposition of CQDs onto the nanotubes. TEM elemental mapping confirmed the presence of K, Ti, O, and C in KT-2.0–300 (Fig. S1 of SD). The elements K, Ti and O are from KTNT, while C originated from the CQDs, indicating the successful decoration of CQDs onto KTNT.

For the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1d), P25-type TiO2 nanoparticles, which were the precursors for synthesis of KTNT, exhibited mixed crystalline phases of rutile (JCPDS card No. 21–1276) and anatase (JCPDS card No. 21–1272) [25]. After undergoing hydrothermal treatment, all TiO2 phases were fully transformed into potassium titanate. The XRD patterns of both neat KTNT and KT-xCQDs-yT were consistent, aligning well with the monoclinic phase of potassium titanate (JCPDS card No. 40–0403, K2Ti6O13). The diffraction angles at 2θ = 11.5°, 24.1°, 29.3°, 33.8°, 43.5°, 47.9° and 66.3° correspond to the (200), (110), (310), (312), (602), (020) and (424) crystal planes, respectively [24]. The absence of additional peaks indicates that no impurities were present in the samples. Furthermore, it is also suggested that the loading of CQDs and calcination in air below 400 °C did not alter the lattice structure of KTNT [38]. Additionally, the characteristic peak of CQDs was not observed in all the KT-xCQDs-yT photocatalysts, likely due to the small amount of CQDs introduced in the composite photocatalysts [8,39].

The surface chemical composition and oxidation states of KTNT and KT-2.0–300 are then analyzed using XPS. The XPS survey scan spectra of both KTNT and KT-2.0–300 reveal the presence of K, Ti, O, and C peaks (Fig. 1e). In comparison to KTNT, KT-2.0–300 shows an increase in atomic proportion of C element by 11.49 %, indicating successful decoration of CQDs onto KTNT. Fig. 1f shows that the binding energy peaks of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 in KTNT were located at 458.3 and 464.1 eV, respectively, corresponding to Ti4+ in the crystal structure [40]. Compared with KTNT, the Ti 2p peaks in KT-2.0–300 exhibited a 0.7 eV shift to the higher binding energy, owing to the change of Ti chemical environment after the interaction of CQDs and KTNT [41]. Fig. 1g displays the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s in KT-2.0–300. The peaks located at 284.8 and 286.4 eV were attributed to CC sp2 hybridized carbon and the C from C–O–C bond of CQDs, while the peak at 288.5 eV was ascribed to the C from OC–O bond [42]. All the above XPS results demonstrated the successful loading of CQDs onto KTNT for KT-2.0–300.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst under light irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a and 2b present the UV–vis DRS spectra of as-prepared materials. Table S2 of SD summarizes the light absorption edge and band gap energy of various materials. Fig. 2a demonstrates that the introduction of CQDs significantly enhanced the light absorption in the 350–700 nm range. The light absorption edge of original KTNT and KT-0–300 was observed at 345.3 and 348.3 nm, with a band gap energy (E g) of 3.59 and 3.56 eV, respectively. After decorated with CQDs, the light absorption edge of KT-2.0–300 shifted to 364.3 nm, with a reduced E g value of 3.40 eV. For the effect of CQDs loading amount, when the loading volume of CQDs was set at 0.5 mL, the band gap of KT-0.5–300 was 3.54 eV and its light absorption capacity is less than that of KT-2.0–300. When the volume of added CQDs reached 2.5 mL, an overabundance of CQDs obstructed the light-absorption sites of KTNT [43], leading to a significant decline in the photocatalytic performance of the material. Fig. 2b shows that the light absorption of the materials experienced slight changes when calcined in air at 200 and 300 °C. However, when the calcination temperature increased to 400 °C, the light absorption was significantly reduced, which is likely due to the destruction of the CQDs and KTNT structure at high temperature [44]. Among the as-prepared photocatalysts (Table S2 of SD), the material with 2.0 mL of CQDs loading and calcination temperature of 300 °C exhibited the most favorable light absorption properties, i.e., the lowest E g value of 3.40 eV. To further evaluate the band structures of KTNT and KT-2.0–300, valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) was performed and results are shown in Fig. 2c. The VB potentials for KTNT and KT-2.0–300 were determined to be 2.87 and 3.17 eV, respectively. Then, the CB of these two photocatalysts were calculated as −0.72 and −0.23 eV, respectively.

The performance of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants is not only related to the light absorption performance of photocatalyst, but also related to the interfacial charge transfer ability [45]. To further illustrate the photocatalytic properties of KT-2.0–300 and KTNT, a series of photoelectric characterizations were conducted. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) result revealed that KT-2.0–300 with a smaller arc radius presented a lower resistance for charge transfer than KTNT (Fig. 2d), and thus presented higher charge transfer efficiency. Fig. 2e displays the amperometric i-t curve (IT) curves of KTNT and KT-2.0–300 under simulated solar light. The photocurrent intensity of KT-2.0–300 was much higher (∼2 times) than that of KTNT, further demonstrating the higher efficiency of charge carrier transfer for KT-2.0–300 with CQDs. Finally, the curves of cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) exhibit that KT-2.0–300 presented a stronger current density and higher redox ability than KTNT, suggesting that the electrical conductivity and photocatalytic activity of KT-2.0–300 were improved after CQDs decoration (Fig. 2f and Fig. S2 of SD).

Based on the E g value from UV–vis DRS and VB level from VB-XPS analysis, a schematic of the band structure for KTNT and KT-2.0–300 was constructed (Fig. S3). The observed enhancement in light absorption and the reduction in the band gap following CQDs modification are anticipated to contribute to improved photocatalytic performance of KTNT [39]. The BET specific surface area, which is closely related to photocatalytic performance, was determined through nitrogen adsorption and desorption experiments conducted at −196 °C. The results indicated that KT-2.0–300 exhibited a slightly lower BET specific surface area of 228.7 m2/g compared to KTNT, which had a surface area of 243.2 m2/g. This reduction is attributed to the introduction of CQDs, which may cover some of inside pores of KTNT [8].

The photocatalytic degradation performance of the as-prepared materials was evaluated by degradation efficiency of NPX under simulated solar light irradiation. The adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the different materials and NPX was achieved within 30 min in dark ( Fig. 3a and 3b). In the photocatalytic degradation experiment of neat KTNT without any modification, it was found that the direct photolysis of NPX was very little (degradation efficiency = 20 %). In addition, CQDs had neither adsorption nor photocatalytic degradation of NPX, with extremely low NPX removal efficiency of < 1 %. Therefore, CQDs mainly acted as an electron mediator and changed the band structure of KTNT, which are discussed in the Section 3.6 in detail. Then, Fig. 3a illustrates the photocatalytic degradation of NPX using various photocatalysts with different CQDs loading contents, but with a consistent calcination temperature of 300 °C. Overall, the loading of CQDs improved the adsorption capacity of NPX by KTNT, because carbon materials such as CQDs can capture organic pollutant through hydrophobic effect and π-π interaction [46]. However, the adsorption performance of the materials was not directly proportional to the amount of CQDs added, because it was not only related to the total amount of CQDs, but also to the dispersion of CQDs and the interfacial contact with the matrix [47]. The non-uniform distribution (observed in the TEM image, Fig. 1c) produced localized areas with different adsorption capacities, explaining the irregular trend. Pre-adsorption enhancement can make NPX enrich on the surface of catalyst, thus the reactive species generated by subsequent photocatalysis can efficiently react with NPX, thereby promoting its degradation efficiency. It is evident that the photocatalyst with a 2.0 mL addition of CQDs solution (KT-2.0–300) demonstrated the highest photocatalytic performance for NPX degradation, achieving 100 % removal efficiency within 20 min. In addition, the rate constant k 1 for KT-2.0–300 (0.2429 min−1) was 52 times higher than that for neat KTNT (0.0047 min−1) and 37 times than KT-0–300 (0.0066 min−1) (Table S3 of SD). In addition, for a low CQDs loading (< 2 mL) on KTNT, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of NPX increased with the increase of the amount of loading CQDs, confirming the important role of CQDs in photocatalytic reaction. However, an excessive CQDs (> 2 mL) would form a relatively crowded space around the catalyst, producing a steric hindrance effect for reaction. Specifically, when the reactant molecules diffused and approached the surface of catalyst, they were hindered by CQDs and had difficulty in reaching the active sites of catalyst, thereby leading to low degradation efficiency [8,39].

Fig. 3b illustrates the photocatalytic degradation of NPX by various photocatalysts prepared at different calcination temperatures, but with a consistent addition of 2.0 mL CQDs solution. The appropriate calcination temperature can optimize the bonding mode of the composite, which can affect the adsorption kinetics [41]. At lower temperatures (< 200 ºC), it is difficult for CQDs to form a stable bond with KTNT [48]; Medium temperatures (e.g., 300 ºC) can optimize the balance between bonding and material porosity of CQDs and KTNTs [43], but higher temperatures (> 400 ºC) may degrade CQDs or destroy the nanotube structures [47]. The material without calcination treatment (KT-2.0–0) demonstrated weak photocatalytic performance, with 73.6 % of NPX degradation efficiency within 30 min and k 1 value of 0.0368 min−1. In comparison, KT-2.0–200 and KT-2.0–300 exhibited greatly enhanced photocatalytic activity (k 1 = 0.0938 and 0.2429 min−1), achieving 100 % NPX removal. However, when the calcination temperature was increased to 400 °C, the photocatalytic performance of KT-2.0–400 dropped drastically, with only 32.8 % of NPX being removed at 30 min and a low k 1 value of 0.0074 min−1. As discussed above, CQDs can extend the range of solar energy utilization and reduce the recombination of electron-hole pairs [49]. When the sample was calcined at 200 °C, the composite likely did not form stable chemical bonds between CQDs and KTNT due to the insufficient temperature, suggesting that the photocatalytic ability could be further enhanced if the calcination temperature was increased. However, at 400 °C, most of the CQDs and KTNT in the composite photocatalysts was destroyed, leading to a significant decrease in photocatalytic performance [44]. In addition, the developed KT-2.0–300 also showed higher photocatalytic reactivity for degradation of NPX compared with other reported catalysts (Table S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS produced in the photocatalysis system and spin polarization effect
ce:section.ce:para: To identify the reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved in the photocatalytic reaction, scavenger quenching experiments were conducted (Fig. 3c). The addition of TI had negligible effect on the NPX degradation, indicating that •O2 - did not contribute to NPX removal. In contrast, the addition of AO and IPA greatly inhibited the photocatalytic reaction, with a more pronounced effect observed for AO. The reaction rate constant k 1 for NPX removal decreased from 0.2429 to 0.1938 min−1 with the addition of AO and further decreased to 0.03 min−1 with the addition of IPA. These findings suggest that photogenerated holes (h⁺) and •OH were the primary ROS driving the photocatalytic degradation of NPX in this system.

EPR analysis was further conducted to identify the produced ROS after photoactivation (Fig. 3d and 3e). The four-line signal with relative intensities of 1:2:2:1 (AN = AH = 14.9 G) assigned to DMPO-•OH [50] was observed in both KTNT and KT-2.0–300 photocatalytic systems, which is consistent with the scavenger quenching experiment (Fig. 3d). The intensity of •OH produced in the KT-2.0–300 system was much higher than that of KTNT, which proved that the introduction of CQDs greatly enhanced the production of active species. h
+ is hard to directly identify through EPR, and the presence of photogenerated h
+ is usually confirmed by detecting the generation of photogenerated e
-
[51]. TEMPO is an oxidizing paramagnetic species, which is often used for e
- capture [52,53]. Thus, the generation of e
- is generally determined by the weakening of TEMPO’s signal, thereby determining the generation of photogenerated h
+ . In both KTNT and KT-2.0–300 systems under dark condition, a strong triplet peak attributed to TEMPO (AN = 16.0 G) was observed (Fig. 3e) [52]. Under solar light irradiation, the TEMPO triplet peak in the KTNT system had hardly changed, while it in the KT-2.0–300 system dropped sharply, indicating that TEMPO had captured e
- , proving the generation and migration of e
- . The generation of an e
- will inevitably leave a h
+ , so the capture of e
- proved that h
+ was generated in the photocatalysis system (Fig. 3e). In addition, superoxide radical (•O2 -) was not detected through EPR in this system (Fig. S4), due to the less negative CB level (-0.23 eV) than the reduction potential of O2/•O2 - (-0.33 eV) [54]. Therefore, the introduction of CQDs not only enhanced the photochemical and electrochemical properties of KTNT, but also enriched the active species in the photocatalytic system, which greatly promoted its efficiency on degradation of pollutants.

Meanwhile, EPR can be used to characterize and analyze solid-phase materials by using the resonance absorption characteristics of electron-unpaired species in a static magnetic field [55]. In the solid-phase EPR test of the material, KT-2.0–300 exhibited a strong spin polarization compared with KTNT (Fig. 3f), due to the distinct peaks at 3508.9 and 3515.0 G [56]. Furthermore, the introduction of an applied magnetic field further verified the enhancement of the photocatalytic performance by spin polarization. As shown in Fig. 3g, when a magnetic field of 12 mT is applied, KT-2.0–300 could degrade 100 % NPX in 10 min with a k 1 value of 0.4726 min−1, which was 2 times faster than without applying a magnetic field (k 1 = 0.2429 min−1). However, there was no change in the degradation efficiency of neat KTNT in the magnetic field at 12 mT, which also corroborated the fact that there was no spin polarization in the neat KTNT in the solid-phase EPR test (Fig. 3f). Therefore, the above results verify that the spin polarization induced by CQDs is the key mechanism for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity, which is consistent with the DFT calculation results ( Fig. 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of water chemistry factors on photocatalytic degradation of NPX
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3h displays the photocatalytic activity of KT-2.0–300 at different pH, highlighting the significant influence of pH on the adsorption and degradation of NPX by KT-2.0–300. The point of zero charge (pHpzc) of KT-2.0–300 was detected as 3.28 (Fig. S5 of SD), and the dissociation species of NPX (pKa = 4.15) in water was presented in Fig. S6 of SD. At pH = 3, adsorption of NPX by KT-2.0–300 was particularly strong. The surface of KT-2.0–300 was positively charged, which can capture neutral NPX molecule through strong electrostatic attraction [57]. However, when the solution pH was higher than 4.15, the surface of material became negatively charged, with an increasing number of negative charges as the pH raised. In addition, NPX would dissociate H+ and existed as a negatively charged ion. Therefore, adsorption of NPX was inhibited due to electrostatic repulsion. Moreover, pH also had a big impact on the photocatalytic reaction. At higher pH, the photocatalytic degradation of NPX was notably inhibited. For example, at pH = 5, NPX was completely degraded within 20 min, while at pH = 11, the degradation efficiency dropped to 37 %.

A variety of ions (e.g., Ca2+, SO4 2-, HCO3 - and PO4 3-) and natural organic matter (e.g., HA) widely exist in real wastewater and have a non-negligible impact on the removal of NPX. However, as can be seen from Fig. S7, the removal efficiency of NPX by KT-2.0–300 developed in this study was still high (all > 90 %) in the case of coexistence of ions and HA. In particular, the degradation efficiency of NPX decreased from 100 % to 93 % after the addition of HCO3 - to the system. According to previous pH tests (Fig. 3h), the optimal pH of for reaction was 5, and HCO3 - was a radical quencher for •OH, which would react with •OH to produce less reactive radicals such as CO3•- [58]. In addition, the addition of HA hindered the degradation efficiency of NPX by 9.0 %. It is because the active species produced in the photocatalytic system of KT-2.0–300 were •OH and h
+ , which would react with HA and be quenched [59]. Furthermore, the effect of initial NPX concentration on the photocatalytic system was also investigated (Fig. S8). The adsorption capacity of KT-2.0–300 for NPX exhibited an inverse correlation with the increasing NPX concentration, attributed to the finite adsorption sites available on the material surface [25]. At lower concentrations of NPX (1 and 3 μM), the system demonstrated high performance, achieving complete NPX degradation within 10 min due to sufficient adsorption sites and generation of abundant reactive species. However, a remarkable reduction in degradation efficiency was observed at elevated concentrations (>5 μM), with removal efficiency dropping to 67.58 % (7 μM) and 28.81 % (10 μM) within 30 min. This deterioration likely stemmed from mass transfer limitation impeding NPX diffusion toward catalytic active sites, coupled with competitive occupation of surface-active sites and light-shielding effects induced by excess NPX molecules, collectively diminishing the photocatalytic activity [60].

The reusability of KT-2.0–300 for the photocatalytic degradation of NPX under simulated solar light is presented in Fig. 3i. The results indicate that KT-2.0–300 exhibited remarkable stability over five reuse cycles. After five consecutive cycles, the NPX removal efficiency only marginally decreased from 100 % to 90.4 %, underscoring the material’s robust reusability. The XPS test of KT-2.0–300 was carried out after five cycles, and it was found that the carbon content contributed only by CQDs did not change, proving that the CQDs were almost not leaching out or degraded during recycling (Fig. S9). Fig. S10 of SD demonstrates that visible light could effectively activate KT-2.0–300 for photocatalytic degradation of NPX. Under simulated solar light, the photocatalytic reaction achieved a 100 % NPX removal efficiency within 20 min. When under visible light, the removal efficiency of NPX also could reach up to 78.8 % at 20 min. In addition, extending the reaction time to 30 min under visible light allowed for the complete removal of NPX. This indicated that while visible light was less efficient than full-spectrum solar irradiation considering the energy of photons, it was still capable of fully degrading NPX with a longer exposure time. These findings suggested that KT-2.0–300 held significant potential for practical application in pharmaceutical contaminants removal from water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance
ce:section.ce:para: To deeply clarify the underlying mechanism on enhanced photocatalytic activity after the decoration of CQDs on KTNT, DFT calculations were further used. Fig. 4a and Fig. S11a present the relevant structure models of CQDs/KTNT and KTNT. Fig. 4b and 4c show CQDs/KTNT possesses much narrower calculated band gap (0.6 eV) than KTNT (2.74 eV), confirming the higher photocatalytic activity of CQDs/KTNT. It is worth noting that although the trend of the band gap obtained by the DFT calculation and UV-DRS is same, a numerical difference is observed. This is due to the following reasons: 1) the theoretical band gap value is derived using a generalized gradient approximation (GGA) functional (e.g., Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof), which systematically underestimates the band gap due to incomplete treatment of electron exchange-related effects; 2) DFT calculations usually focus on the electronic structure of the ground state, without considering exciton interactions, which could significantly affect UV-DRS spectra; 3) UV-DRS measurements are sensitive to surface defects and adsorbed species, whereas the DFT model in this study assumes an ideal bulk crystal structure. Meanwhile, the more negative CB than neat KTNT in Fig. 4b and 4c also prove the electrons in Ti-3d orbital has higher activity.

Projected density of state (PDOS) calculations were further performed to reveal the influence of introduced CQDs on the electronic structure of KTNT (Fig. 4d and 4e). In general, the asymmetry between the spin-up and spin-down of PDOS represents the spin polarization [56]. In CQDs/KTNT, the electrons in Ti-3d exhibit significant spin polarization (Fig. 4d), which is consistent with previous solid EPR test (Fig. 3f) and applied magnetic field experiments (Fig. 3g). Spin polarization makes electrons and holes have different spin states, and according to Pauli’s incompatibility principle, the electrons and holes with opposite spins are difficult to reunite, which significantly prolongs the lifetime of carriers [61]. The spin transport channel generated by spin polarization is conducive to the rapid migration of electrons and holes to the active site on the surface of the catalyst and participate in the redox reaction [55]. In addition, spin polarization can change the electron cloud distribution of the active sites on the surface of the catalyst, thereby enhancing the adsorption capacity of reactant molecules [62], which is also consistent with previous experimental results (Fig. 3a and 3b). Moreover, compared with KTNT, the CBM of Ti-3d orbital contribution in CQDs/KTNT moves to a more negative energy level as displayed in Fig. 4b-e, indicating Ti-3d is more receptive to electrons from VB, thus promoting the photogenerated electrons transfer to outside and subsequent ROS generation. The projected density of states (PDOS) for CQDs/KTNT in Fig. 4d and 4e indicate that, besides O-2p, C-2p also contributes to the VB near the Fermi level compared with the original KTNT. Therefore, the decoration of CQDs regulates the electronic structure of KTNT near the Fermi level and thus improves electronic reaction activity in the photocatalysis process. In addition, the C-2p orbital exists between O-2p and Ti-3d orbital for CQDs/KTNT, suggesting that the introduction of CQDs is linked to a bond. Thus, the CQDs can accept electrons from O-2p and then transfer to Ti-3d, leading to improved transfer efficiency of electrons.

Fig. 4f and 4g show that the work function of CQDs/KTNT (Φ = 3.22 eV) is much lower than that of neat KTNT (Φ = 6.66 eV), indicating the electrons in CQDs/KTNT can overcome smaller energy and be transferred to the outside for further reaction. Moreover, the electron density in Figs. S11b and S11c of SD displays the electron density of Ti site in CQDs/KTNT is apparently higher than that in KTNT, proving the electron density of Ti-3d is improved after CQDs decoration, which is beneficial to the photocatalytic reaction. The spin density in Fig. 4h and Fig. S11d further demonstrates the Ti-3d orbital produces spin electrons after CQDs decoration, which can generate a spin channel and accelerate the electrons transfer between O-2p and Ti-3d during the photocatalytic reaction. Finally, the charge density difference (CDD) in Fig. 4h (inset figure) suggests that the electrons are more likely to transfer from CQDs to KTNT in CQDs/KTNT, leading to accumulation of electrons on KTNT. The Bader charge further indicates 1.06 e
– is transferred from CQDs to KTNT, thus strong interaction exists between CQDs and KTNT, and KTNT acts as an electronic memory, leading to a high e
– utilization rate and high reactivity. To conclude, the decoration of CQDs greatly promotes the photocatalytic activity, which regulates electronic structure near the Fermi level, narrows the band gap, and generates spin polarization and spin electrons, thus improving the transfer efficiency of electrons and inhibiting the recombination of charge carriers [55].

Based on the revealed photocatalytic mechanism, the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs-decorated KTNT composite material is illustrated in Fig. 5 and Fig. S3. In comparison, the VB potential of KT-2.0–300 (3.17 eV) is higher than that of KTNT (2.87 eV). This higher oxidative potential is a key factor in improving the photocatalytic degradation of NPX [63]. Consequently, when photons are captured by the material for photogenerated electrons transfer, the oxidation ability of the formed h⁺ on the VB of KT-2.0–300 is stronger than that of KTNT (Eqs. 3 and 4). Meanwhile, the h
+ will keep opposite direction compared with e
– due to the spin polarization of KT-2.0–300, thus preventing the recombination of e
– –h⁺ pairs [61]. Then, h⁺ will react with H2O to produce •OH (Eq. 5). •OH further attack the NPX molecule, causing its degradation and even mineralization (Eq. 6). The improved photocatalytic performance of KTNT after CQDs decoration can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, the modification enhances light absorption (especially at visible light regions), thus narrowing the band gap [16,64]. Secondly, due to their conjugated carbon structure, CQDs serve as superior electron acceptors, reservoirs, and transfer agents. This allows them to effectively trap electrons generated by KTNT during light irradiation, thereby reducing the recombination of photogenerated e
– –h⁺ pairs within the nano-photocatalyst [18,65]. Additionally, the introduction of CQDs improves the Ti 3d orbital in KTNT, so that the electrons on it are in a spin polarization state, which further inhibits the e
– –h⁺ pairs recombination and enhances the photocatalytic performance [55,61]. However, it is important to note that an excessive amount of CQDs can cause a shielding effect, which inhibits the photocatalytic degradation of NPX [8]
(3) KTNT + hv → h
+ + e
- (excitation)
(4) CQDs + e
- → CQDs–e
- (electron transfer)
(5)
h
+ + H2O → •OH + H+
(6) •OH + NPX → degradation intermediates → CO2 + H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation pathway and toxicity evaluation of NPX in the photocatalysis system
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation pathway of NPX by KT-2.0–300 under solar simulator irradiation was elucidated using a combination of DFT calculation and LC-MS analysis. The degradation intermediates detected by LC-MS are listed in Table S4 of SD. To predict the reactive sites for radical attack during the photocatalytic reaction, natural population analysis (NPA) charge distribution and the Fukui index (f -) were calculated considering the primary ROS (•OH) is a strong electrophilic species. These results indicate specific atoms (C1, C2, C5, C9, C10, and C13) on the NPX molecule have relatively larger f - values ( Fig. 6).

The proposed degradation pathway of NPX, as supported by detected intermediates and Fukui index calculations, contains three primary routes: demethoxylation, decarboxylation, and dehydroxylation ( Fig. 7a) [66,67]. Initially, •OH preferentially attack the C13 site of NPX, characterized by a higher Fukui index (f - = 0.0714), facilitating the removal of the methoxy group (–OCH3) from the naphthalene ring, thereby yielding compound A (m/z = 217.11303, t R = 4.55 min), wherein a hydroxyl group (–OH) would replace the –OCH3. Subsequent hydroxylation at the C9 position converted compound A into compound B (m/z = 233.11090, t R = 4.40 min), following a •OH-mediated attack at C10, leading to formation of compound C (m/z = 249. 13909, t R = 3.70 min). The removal of the 2-methyl acetic acid group (–CH(CH3)COOH) in both compound B and compound C occurred through •OH attack at the C1 position, producing compounds D (m/z = 193.13179, t R = 6.47 min) and E (m/z = 177.07789, t R = 5.50 min). Notably, compound D may also arise from hydroxylation at the naphthalene ring of compound E [67]. In the decarboxylation pathway, an initial •OH attack at C22 prompted the cleavage of the carboxyl group (–COOH), resulting in the formation of compound F (m/z = 185.10876, t R = 6.30 min), which underwent an addition reaction to yield compound H (m/z = 235.10034, t R = 1.73 min) [66]. Meanwhile, the dehydroxylation pathway involved the elimination of –OH from the –COOH to produce compound G (m/z = 215.11351, t R = 6.30 min), which was then hydroxylated by •OH to form compound H. During this transition, the carbonyl group (–CO) was replaced by an –OH group [68].

The toxicity of NPX and its degradation products was analyzed by means of Toxicity Estimation Software Tool (T.E.S.T.). The developmental toxicity and oral rat LD50 test were obtained based on quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) method [69]. Fig. 7b indicates that the developed photocatalytic oxidation process can dramatically reduce the developmental toxicity after degradation, where NPX was considered as a developmental toxicant pollutant (value of 0.91), while some products have been reduced to developmental non-toxicant state (products D/E/F, value <0.5). After reaction, the oral rat LD50 values for all the detected degradation products were reduced (Fig. 7c). Therefore, the proposed photocatalytic degradation process is a green technology for organic matter degradation with universal toxicity attenuation.

In this study, we report a facile method to synthesize CQDs-decorated KTNT composite photocatalysts, which were achieved through an initial hydrothermal process followed by a calcination. The CQDs were successfully anchored onto the KTNT without altering their crystal structure. Notably, the decoration of CQDs markedly improved the photocatalytic activity of KTNT. However, an excess of CQDs was found to impede light absorption by the nanotubes, and elevated temperatures were detrimental to structure of CQDs and KTNT, both of which inhibited the photocatalytic activity of the composite photocatalysts. Among the variants, KT-2.0–300 exhibited the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency for NPX, with a reaction rate constant k 1 that was ∼52 times greater than that of neat KTNT. Through applied magnetic field experiments, EPR measurements and DFT calculations, the enhanced performance is mainly attributed to the rapid separation of electron-hole pairs rate induced by spin polarization. Quenching experiments and EPR analysis confirmed that h
+ and •OH were the dominant ROS in the NPX degradation process. The photocatalytic degradation pathways of NPX contained demethoxylation, decarboxylation and dehydroxylation, leading to detoxification of NPX. KT-2.0–300 maintained high efficacy over five consecutive reuse cycles, underscoring its potential as a promising CQDs/KTNT composite photocatalyst for the remediation of emerging contaminants in wastewater.

Weiliang Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Software-DFT calculation, Writing-Original Draft; Zhenyu He: Investigation, Data curation, Validation; Qiankun Chen: Investigation, Data curation, Validation; Xudong Yang: Software-DFT calculation, Data curation; Long Chen: Software-DFT calculation, Investigation, Validation; Jun Duan: Data curation, Validation; Haodong Ji: Resources, Investigation, Supervision, Writing-Review & Editing; Wen Liu: Resources, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
